coredata.dc:title: Measurement and evaluation of anisotropic thermophysical parameters of lithium-ion battery electrode stack: An experimental and numerical study

coredata.dc:description: The development of advanced electrode materials and their complex formulations has made it increasingly difficult to obtain accurate thermophysical parameters of the active zone in lithium-ion cells. These parameters, such as thermal conductivity and specific heat capacity, are crucial for optimizing the performance and safety of the battery. Conventional methods for obtaining these measurements often require expensive and sophisticated laboratory equipment, which limits accessibility and ease of use. An innovative hybrid approach is presented for measuring the thermophysical parameters of the active zone in lithium-ion batteries. This method combines experimental measurements with numerical simulations to determine anisotropic thermal conductivity, specific heat capacity, and the density of the electrode stack. A key aspect of this approach is the use of low-viscosity liquid paraffin to simulate the effects of the electrolyte. The through-plane and in-plane thermal conductivities of both wetted and dry specimens are measured, while the specific heat capacity is approximated numerically. This simple, cost-effective technique eliminates the need for specialized and expensive lab equipment. The through-plane thermal conductivity of the wetted specimen was found to be 2 orders of magnitude greater than that of the dry specimen, while the difference between the in-plane thermal conductivities of the wetted and dry specimens was negligible. The errors in the measured values of through-plane and in-plane thermal conductivities were approximately 4% and 2%, respectively, while the numerically approximated specific heat capacity showed an error of around 2.5%. All measured parameters were found to be within reported ranges. A 3D lumped thermal model incorporating the measured thermophysical parameters was simulated using the commercial software ANSYS Fluent to examine the effects of thermal anisotropy. The simulation results were validated against experimental data and were found to be in good agreement.

head.ce:title: Measurement and evaluation of anisotropic thermophysical parameters of lithium-ion battery electrode stack: An experimental and numerical study

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The development of advanced electrode materials and their complex formulations has made it increasingly difficult to obtain accurate thermophysical parameters of the active zone in lithium-ion cells. These parameters, such as thermal conductivity and specific heat capacity, are crucial for optimizing the performance and safety of the battery. Conventional methods for obtaining these measurements often require expensive and sophisticated laboratory equipment, which limits accessibility and ease of use. An innovative hybrid approach is presented for measuring the thermophysical parameters of the active zone in lithium-ion batteries. This method combines experimental measurements with numerical simulations to determine anisotropic thermal conductivity, specific heat capacity, and the density of the electrode stack. A key aspect of this approach is the use of low-viscosity liquid paraffin to simulate the effects of the electrolyte. The through-plane and in-plane thermal conductivities of both wetted and dry specimens are measured, while the specific heat capacity is approximated numerically. This simple, cost-effective technique eliminates the need for specialized and expensive lab equipment. The through-plane thermal conductivity of the wetted specimen was found to be 2 orders of magnitude greater than that of the dry specimen, while the difference between the in-plane thermal conductivities of the wetted and dry specimens was negligible. The errors in the measured values of through-plane and in-plane thermal conductivities were approximately 4% and 2%, respectively, while the numerically approximated specific heat capacity showed an error of around 2.5%. All measured parameters were found to be within reported ranges. A 3D lumped thermal model incorporating the measured thermophysical parameters was simulated using the commercial software ANSYS Fluent to examine the effects of thermal anisotropy. The simulation results were validated against experimental data and were found to be in good agreement.

ce:section.ce:para: Despite the promise of lithium-ion batteries in electric propulsion applications, achieving speed and mileage comparable to those of the internal combustion engine (ICE) remains a challenge. The major obstacle in overcoming this challenge is the temperature sensitivity of lithium-ion batteries. The optimum operating temperature window for the majority of lithium-ion battery systems is 15 ◦C to 35 ◦C [1–4]. There has been an ever-increasing challenge of material sustainability and depletion of fossil fuels[5–9]. Higher temperature could not only deteriorate cell performance but also trigger a chain of exothermic reactions leading to thermal runaway and ultimately to fire or explosion. In electric vehicle (EV) battery packs, a large number of cells are closely packed together. It is essential to maintain each of the cell temperatures within a safe operating range using an effective cooling setup referred to generally as the battery thermal management system (TMS) [10–15]. Designing an efficient TMS requires in-depth knowledge of the cell thermal responses under variable loads and operating conditions. With the advancement in computational techniques, modeling and simulation have frequently been used for thermal characterization of lithium-ion batteries[16–19]. Lumped thermal models are the most commonly used battery models that treat electrode layers as a composite solid with distinct thermal and physical properties. Thermal and physical parameters are the backbone of these models, significantly impacting the accuracy of predictions. Most of the models reported in the published work use thermophysical property data derived from literature [20–22]. The majority of these research articles refer to thermophysical properties of the complete cell, including passive zones. Research articles devoted exclusively to thermophysical parameter evaluation of the cell active zone (electrode-separator layers) are seldom found in the literature [23].

In lithium-ion batteries, the active zone is constituted by anode-separator-cathode layers called a repeat unit. A single repeat unit is spirally wound, or multiple repeat units are stacked together to form cylindrical or flat cell configurations, respectively. Multiple layers offer higher thermal resistance across the layered structure (through-plane direction) due to material discontinuity and the presence of interfaces between the layers. A lower thermal resistance is observed along the in-plane direction affected by the absence of interfaces and discontinuities. In cylindrical cell configurations, in-plane and through-plane correspond to axial and radial directions, respectively. Different thermal resistances in the 2 directions impart thermal anisotropy within the cell active zone (referred to generally as the jellyroll). Due to the temperature sensitivity of lithium-ion batteries, it is essential to account for the thermal anisotropy while modeling the thermal behavior of the individual cells. The accuracy of lithium-ion battery models relies heavily on the accurate knowledge of thermal and physical properties of jellyroll and cell components. Moreover, efficient TMS designs for EV battery packs could not be affected without prior knowledge of thermal and physical parameters of the cell [24–26]. Thermal and physical property data of cell construction and electrode materials are seldom provided by the manufacturer[27–29]. Lithium-ion cell components like cell casing, current collectors, and insulation are made of common materials, that is, steel, PVC, aluminum, and copper, for which thermophysical property data can easily be obtained. Nonetheless, due to manufacturer confidentiality and the proprietary nature of the cell electrode materials, acquiring reliable information regarding the thermophysical properties of the cell active zone remains a challenge. Numerous numerical and experimental approaches for thermophysical parameterization of lithium-ion cells have been proposed by researchers.

Broadly, 2 approaches are used to measure thermal and physical properties of lithium-ion cell active material and components. A destructive approach that requires the cell to be dismantled for the individual component materials to be characterized. Component material properties are then used to find the global thermophysical properties. Non-destructive approach, on the other hand, does not require the cell to be disassembled. Experimental data is generated, which is later used to calibrate thermal properties using numerical models [30,31].

The most common method to determine the specific heat capacity of lithium-ion battery active material is based on calorimetry [32–35]. Conventional calorimetric measurement methods are extended to advanced forms like accelerated rate calorimetry (ARC) and isothermal battery calorimetry (IBC) [36–39]. In addition to the standard calorimetric techniques, customized calorimetric methods are used that account for thermal variations due to chemical reactions using the energy conservation principle [40,41]. These methods are often expensive due to higher equipment costs [42]. Furthermore, in some instances, larger measurement errors are reported in heat capacity using ARC [43,44]. Another simple and common approach is to use the weighted sum of specific heat capacities of the cell constituting materials. Accuracy of this method, however, relies on exact knowledge of the cell active material composition (anode, cathode, separator, etc.) and corresponding heat capacities of the constituting materials. Moreover, thermal characterization of the constituting materials requires disassembling of the cell to draw the materials, which is often tedious and time-consuming. Another drawback is the exclusion of contact resistance between the electrode layers, which negatively impacts the accuracy[45–47].

Thermal conductivity of the cell active material is reported to be determined through different simplified experimental approaches [48–50]. Thermal conductivity is also measured as the weighted average of the composing materials, neglecting the effect of contacts between jellyroll and cell tabs as well as that of the contact resistance between the electrode layers [51]. Some researchers use serial and parallel thermal resistances to approximate thermal conductivity [52–54]. Neglecting thermal contact resistance between electrode and separator layers, a through-plane component of thermal conductivity could be over- or underpredicted by this method [55].

Use of impedance spectroscopy to measure thermal properties of lithium-ion cells has also been reported [56–58]. Thermal impedance curves are fitted to the cell response curves against an excitation signal. This method suffers a drawback of being sluggish due to low frequency of the cell response. The lumped capacitance method was proposed to estimate the average heat capacity of a pouch lithium-ion cell [36,59,60]. In this method battery was assumed to be a composite body with specific thermal resistance and capacitance. This method has limitations when applied to larger cell configurations [40]. A comparatively simple experimental setup was used to find thermal properties of pouch and cylindrical lithium-ion cells using an external heat source. The main advantage of this method is the use of a single experiment to measure the cross-plane component of thermal conductivity and specific heat capacity [24,61]. A major drawback, however, is associated with the use of a vacuum that requires extra provisions in the experimental setup.

Battery operating and state parameters, for example, temperature, state of charge (SOC), and state of health (SOH), are expected to have a significant impact on thermal properties of the cell. A comprehensive review of different experimental approaches used to find specific heat capacity and thermal conductivity of cells and components was carried out, analyzing the impact of operating conditions and cell states on the measured values [62]. Inconsistencies between specific heat capacities of jellyroll and that of the full cell were found. It was identified that the effect of contact resistance and dependencies on battery state has not been adequately investigated. The effect of cell operating temperature and compression pressure on through-plane thermal conductivity of a prismatic NMC lithium-ion cell was investigated [63]. Higher external pressure was found to have increased thermal conductivity due to a reduction in contact resistance between the electrode layers caused by the compression. Higher temperature, on the other hand, caused a linear reduction in thermal conductivity. In addition to the experimental approaches, numerical simulations in combination with experiments were also used for thermal parameterization of lithium-ion cells. Thermal properties were approximated by fitting numerically produced temperature profiles to the experimentally generated curves [64–67]. Use of sophisticated experimental setups and 3D simulation models for numerical approximations renders these methods costly, time-consuming, and computationally expensive [67,68].

As indicated by the literature cited above, most of the non-destructive methods, like calorimetry and extended forms, used to measure thermal properties of lithium-ion batteries require expensive equipment and a specially designed experimental setup. The majority of the destructive techniques disregard the effect of electrolyte and consider dry electrode layers while approximating battery thermal properties. Thermal anisotropy has not been addressed in most of the studies, and only the radial or through-plane component of thermal conductivity has been measured. The density of electrolyte-soaked jellyroll has seldom been reported in the published literature. A single study dedicated to the measurement of all the thermal and physical parameters of jellyroll (e.g., anisotropic thermal conductivity, heat capacity, and density) is not reported in the published literature. A detailed review of the published research articles indicates 2 major research gaps regarding lithium-ion battery thermophysical parameter estimation. Measurement inaccuracies caused by the loss of electrolyte during the dismantling of the cell need further investigation. Measurement techniques for the anisotropic thermal conductivity of lithium-ion cell active zones need to be refined further. Moreover, measuring all the parameters simultaneously would help save time, effort, and cost. These gaps have also been identified in the recently published review articles on lithium-ion battery thermophysical parameter estimation [40,62].

The present work is based on a novel approach to find lumped thermal and physical properties of the lithium-ion battery active zone. The parameterization procedure follows a hybrid strategy that uses experimental measurements in combination with numerical simulations. Standard laboratory equipment was used to measure the anisotropic thermal conductivity of the jellyroll. Dry electrode-separator layers were soaked with liquid paraffin to simulate the effect of electrolyte. The Guarded Comparative Longitudinal Heat Flow method was used to find the thermal conductivity of the specimens using 304 L stainless steel as the standard reference material. Heat capacity of the jellyroll was approximated by fitting the simulated temperature curves to the transient temperature curves obtained from thermal conductivity measurements. A 2D axisymmetric model was solved using finite element (FE) approximation to produce temperature response curves. To the best of our knowledge, the present approach has never been used before and, hence, would help improve the understanding of thermal transport in lithium-ion batteries, essential for thermally efficient cell and TMS designs.

Anisotropic thermal conductivity of the jellyroll was measured experimentally. A steady state Guarded Comparative Longitudinal heat flow technique based on ASTM standard (Designation: E 1225-04) is used to measure anisotropic thermal conductivity of lithium-ion cell active material (jellyroll). This method is best suited for measurement of thermal conductivity of homogeneous-opaque solids having thermal conductivity between 0.2 and 200 [W∙m−1∙K−1] over an approximate temperature range of 90 K to 1300 K. This method can be used outside these ranges but only at the expense of accuracy [69,70]. A procedure for the determination of the anisotropic thermal conductivity of a lithium-ion flat cell electrode stack is presented in this section. Heat capacity was evaluated numerically and presented in the section to follow, along with an approximation of the mass density.

Electrolytes used in most of the modern lithium-ion battery technologies are composed of materials that are hazardous and toxic in nature. Exposure in any form could cause milder to serious health issues [71,72]. Hence, for safety reasons, a dried (or electrolyte-free) electrode stack was used for the measurements. Dry electrodes can either be obtained directly from the manufacturer or by leaving the wet electrodes for drying. For that purpose, the cell has to be carefully opened to unpack the electrolyte-laden electrodes. Electrodes are expected to take 4 to 6 weeks until most of the electrolyte dries off. In the present work, a flat lithium-ion cell configuration was used for thermophysical parametrization of the active zone. Nevertheless, the same procedure can be used for other configurations with some modifications. This test method requires the measurement specimen to be in a cylindrical shape. Specimens used for the measurements of in-plane and through-plane thermal conductivity of flat cell electrode stack, along with the corresponding reference probes, are shown in Fig. 1.

Two different cylindrical-shaped specimens were prepared to measure the in-plane (k
z ) and through-plane (k
r ) thermal conductivity of the flat cell active zone. Generally, flat cell configuration is described by the Cartesian coordinate system in which in-plane thermal conductivity is constituted by 2 components, that is, k
z and k
y , such that k
z = k
y . Whereas through-plane thermal conductivity constitutes a single component, that is, k
x . Since cylindrical specimens were used in the measurements, in-plane and through-plane components were measured as k
z and k
r , respectively. Either of the 2 specimens can be used for heat capacity and density measurements (Sections 3 and 4). The procedure used to prepare the specimens is described below:

Stainless steel AISI 304L was used as a standard reference material for which thermal conductivity versus temperature data was employed instead of using a constant value as normally practiced. Thermal conductivity data in Table 1 were derived from reference [73]. Standard reference probes used to measure in-plane and through-plane components of thermal conductivity are shown in Figs. 1a and b, respectively:

Average of the 2 temperatures recorded at the specimen sensor locations, that is, T4 and T5 (Fig. 3b) was used as reference temperature. T

ref
were calculated using Eqs. (1) and (2) respectively. Eq. (2) was obtained from a linear fit to the thermal conductivity versus temperature data presented in Table 1.

Electrodes used in lithium-ion batteries contain micropores that constitute a significant part of the electrode volume. Dry electrodes in the absence of electrolyte trap air within the voids and between the electrode layers. Since air is a poor conductor of heat, using dry electrodes could lead to erroneous results regarding thermal conductivity and other parameters. This effect is more dominant in the case of through-plane (k
r ) measurements, where trapped air between the electrode layers crosses the heat flow path. To remedy this problem, low-viscosity liquid paraffin (CAS 8042-47-5) was used to mirror the thermal effects of the electrolyte. Liquid paraffin was used, firstly, because it is inert and safe to handle and secondly, its thermal properties are quite analogous to most of the organic solvents [50–52] used in lithium-ion batteries e.g., ethylene carbonate (EC), dimethyl carbonate (DMC), and diethyl carbonate (DEC). A comparison of thermophysical properties of these solvents and those of the liquid paraffin is presented in Table 2. Since the thickness of electrode and separator layers is on the scale of micrometers, the effect of the difference in thermal characteristics of actual electrolyte and liquid paraffin can justifiably be ignored. The main purpose of wetting is to expel the trapped air in electrode voids. Two methods were used for wetting of the specimen, that is, vacuum furnace and liquid immersion.

Wetting was carried out by pressing the specimen (prepared for through-plane thermal conductivity measurements) using a screw-press to partially remove the trapped air. The pressed specimen was then put under vacuum at about 50°C in a vacuum furnace. Low-viscosity paraffin oil was applied on the sides of the specimen so that it could fill the voids as air is removed under vacuum. The specimen was kept under negative pressure until no bubbles were seen originating from the sides of the specimen where paraffin oil was applied. This method was found to be inefficient in fully expelling the trapped air. Wetting was found to be achieved only near the radial boundaries, leaving most of the inner part of the specimen dry, as shown in Fig. 2a.

Alternately, the specimen was kept immersed in low-viscosity paraffin oil at atmospheric pressure until no bubbles were seen originating from the specimen, indicating that all the air had been removed from the electrode layers. The specimen was then carefully removed from the bath and pressed gently to remove the excess oil. The wetted specimen was left for 24 hours so that the oil gets absorbed in the pores. Immersion as well as drying time should be carefully adjusted so as to remove the excess oil between the electrode layers but to avoid drying of the specimen. Different states of oil penetration into the electrode stack (i.e., partial, over, and normal wetting states) are shown Fig. 2. Thermal conductivity of the specimen is apparently a function of wetting time and applied pressure. This aspect needs further investigation and is subject to future studies. Since air does not directly cross the heat flow path, contact resistance due to trapped air between the electrode layers in the case of in-plane measurements is not as significant as that in the case of through-plane measurements. To improve thermal contact between the specimen and reference probes, thermally conductive silicon paste OMEGATHERM “201” was applied between the specimen and reference probes, which effectively fills the voids and improves thermal contact.

The experimental setup used for the measurements, along with the schematic diagram, is shown in Fig. 3. It consists of a basic measurement assembly attached to heating and cooling units, thermal sensor bank, and data acquisition system. Schematic of the measurement assembly along with sensor locations is shown in the Fig. 3b. Actual assembly unit with reference probes and the measurement specimen is shown in Fig. 3a. The convention (nomenclature and symbols) shown in Fig. 3b is used in the subsequent calculations of thermal conductivity of the specimens, k
S . T1 through T8 represent the temperatures measured at different sensor locations using fine wire T-type temperature sensors from Omega Engineering, Inc. X1, X2, and X3 represent distances between the 2 sensors attached to the top reference probe, specimen, and bottom reference prob, respectively. k
ref is the thermal conductivity of standard reference probes (SS 304L). Eq. (3) is the basic equation used for the calculation of thermal conductivity (k
S ) of the specimens.

The experiment started by installing the thermocouples at the given locations (Fig. 3b). Insulation was applied to the assembly unit, followed by the adjustment of heater and cooler temperature set points. Data were continuously recorded in the data acquisition system until steady state was reached. Steady state temperatures measured at sensor locations T2 through T7 (Fig. 3b) along with separation between the sensors X1, X2, and X3 were plugged into Eq. (3) to find thermal conductivity, k

About −20°C to 80°C temperature window for cooler and heater set points was used in the measurements. Experimental or measurement error was calculated using Eq. (4) through Eq. (8).

In the experimental setup shown in Fig. 3, temperature varies along the length, that is, through sensor locations T2–T7. For accurate measurements, temperature should be fixed since thermal conductivity is a function of temperature. This induces an error in thermal conductivity measurements. Despite all the precautionary measures, thermal contact between the thermal sensors and specimen is not perfect, causing slight fluctuations in sensor output. These factors could impact the accuracy of measurements and have been taken into account as possible sources of error during calculations. T

Specific heat capacity (c

The method used to evaluate c
P of the above specimens was initially proposed by Loew and Merten [83]. Any one of the specimens used to measure in-plane or through-plane components of thermal conductivity could be used to evaluate specific heat capacity. Hence, the specimen used to measure through-plane thermal conductivity, that is, k
r was used to approximate c
P . A 2D axisymmetric model of the measurement assembly unit (Fig. 3a) was used to evaluate the specific heat of the given specimen. The model was implemented into ANSYS Mechanical software package. A geometry model of the measurement assembly was developed using ANSYS built-in CAD tools. The simulation domain was discretised using a finite element (FE) mesh as shown in Fig. 4. In order to generate transient temperature profiles for all the sensors (T1 through T8), the experimental setup shown in Fig. 3 was used. The cooling plate or heat sink (shown in Fig. 3b) was set at 20 °C, and the whole system was allowed to settle at thermal equilibrium. When the system achieved steady state, a preheated stream, at 80°C was introduced abruptly into the top part of the setup, that is, heating plate. The system was again allowed to settle at a thermal equilibrium state.

Temperature was continuously measured at all the sensor locations, that is, T1 through T8. The idea was to obtain the transient temperature curves and fit to these curves the FE simulation data. Simulations were carried out with different heat capacity values of the specimen. Specific heat capacity of the specimen was adjusted in a manner to obtain the best fit of the curves as shown in Fig. 5.

The majority of the lithium-ion battery lumped thermal models disregard heat generation due to ohmic resistance within the passive zone. Moreover, the passive zone comprises common materials (i.e., copper, steel, aluminum, etc.) for which thermal and physical properties can easily be obtained. Hence, passive zone parameterization is out of the scope of the present work. As mentioned before, the lithium-ion cell active zone comprises an anode (negative electrode) and cathode (positive electrode) layers separated by a membrane separator immersed in an electrolyte solution. During discharging and charging, electrochemical reactions occur within the active zone, generating heat, causing the cell temperature to increase. Heat generated within the active zone is due mainly to polarization (activation and concentration) resistance, entropy, and electrolyte resistance [84]. Accurate knowledge of the thermophysical properties of this region is important for efficient heat removal and cooling system designs. This section deals with the results regarding active zone thermophysical parameters (Section 2) along with a detailed analysis.

Thermal conductivity was measured. In order to study the effect of electrolyte and contact resistance, first measurements were carried out using dry specimens (i.e., without wetting with liquid paraffin) at different reference temperatures. Three measurements for each reference temperature were carried out, and an average of the 3 is presented. Two reference temperatures (i.e., 30°C and 50°C) were used for each for the dry and wetted specimens. These reference temperature values were obtained by appropriately adjusting the cooler and heater temperature set points. Since the reference temperature for each measurement was taken as the average of the temperatures recorded at 2 sensor locations on each probe, values were rounded off to 30 °C and 50 °C. Exact measured reference temperatures were listed in Sections 3.1.1 and 3.1.2. Rounded values of the reference temperatures are presented in Table 2.

A summary of the measured values of anisotropic thermal conductivities for dry and wetted specimens of active material is presented in Table 3 along with the corresponding estimated error. Since measurements using dry specimens were just for comparison purposes, error has not been calculated for the dry samples. Moreover, due to the absence of electrolyte and higher contact resistance, a large error should be expected while using dry specimens.

Density is an important physical parameter essential for transient thermal simulations of lithium-ion batteries. Density can be approximated using the volume fraction technique, provided the density of the constituting materials of the cell active zone is known. A single repeat unit comprises copper and aluminum current collectors, anode and cathode active materials, and separator, as shown in Fig. 6. Individual component densities are multiplied by the corresponding volume fractions and summed together to obtain the overall density of the active zone. Since 2 dimensions within the repeat unit are fixed, that is, length and height. The only variable dimension within the repeat unit is thickness, hence individual volume fractions could be replaced by component thicknesses to compute overall density of the active zone. Referring to Fig. 6, the overall density of the active zone (ρ
active ) can be calculated using Eq. (10). This method can also be used to compute specific heat capacity (c
P ) and thermal conductivity (k) of the active zone [85]. Nonetheless, this method is tedious and warrants significant effort and time as compared to the direct measurement technique used in the present work. Furthermore, depending on porosity of the electrode active materials, electrolyte constitutes a significant part of the active zone which is completely disregarded in volume fraction method:

Hence, in the present work, the direct method was used to measure the density of the active zone. The mass of the specimen used in the measurements of thermal conductivity (Section 2.1.4) was determined using the standard lab balance. Either of the 2 specimens, that is, specimen used for in-plane or through-plane thermal conductivity measurements could be used to find density.

As described in Section 2.2, experimentally generated transient temperature response curves for multiple sensor locations (Fig. 3b) were obtained during thermal conductivity measurements (Section 2.1.4). To these curves, simulated temperature profiles generated using the same conditions as those used during the experimental measurements were fitted by adjusting the heat capacity of the jellyroll. Since anisotropic thermal conductivity and density were already determined (Sections 2.1 and 2.3), the transient heat equation was solved for the heat capacity of the active zone. Simulations were repeated with different heat capacities until the best fit was obtained. The best fit was obtained at a heat capacity value of 1075 (J∙kg−1∙K−1) for the active zone. It can be observed from Fig. 5 that while adjusting heat capacity using temperature profile fitting, a change in heat capacity within about 50 (J∙kg−1∙K−1) is difficult to observe. A careful examination of curves revealed that about 2.5% error should be considered in the heat capacity quoted above. Furthermore, as the method used is merely an approximation, about 25 (J∙kg−1∙K−1) should be given and taken. Deviation was computed based on the error in thermal conductivity measurements (Table 2). Nevertheless, the method gives a reasonably good approximation of the active zone c
P using a simple and cost-effective approach.

As a result of intensive research in the field of lithium-ion battery materials, new advanced materials are being developed at an ever-increasing pace. A variety of additives are used in lithium-ion battery electrode materials in order to enhance their electrical and thermal properties. Most of the electrode formulations are confidential, and a lack of authentic information regarding the composition and characterization of these electrodes makes it difficult. Knowledge of mere electrode chemistry proves insufficient in this regard. Therefore, a wide range of thermal and physical properties are reported in the literature for the individual cell chemistries, making it difficult to independently authenticate the measured values. Nevertheless, a comparison could be made with the reported values to verify if the measured data confines within the reported ranges. In Table 4, a comparison of the measured parameters with those reported in the published literature is presented. It is important to emphasize that the present work should be treated as a proposed methodology for thermophysical parameterization of lithium-ion cell active zone rather than a reference to the individual parameter values and cell chemistry.

Due to a lack of authentic information regarding the chemistry of the cell stack used, an educated advanced Delphi was made regarding the cell chemistry based on the data presented in Table 4. NMC (Nickel-Manganese-Cobalt-Oxide) was designated as the most likely candidate, which was further verified using thermal simulations and validating the results. A 3D lumped thermal model was developed for an NMC 21700 cylindrical cell using the predetermined thermophysical parameters (Sections 3.1, 3.2, and 3.3). Commercial CFD software package ANSYS Fluent was used to implement the model. Spatially constant and temporally varying heat generation rate derived from [89] was applied as transient table boundary conditions. This approach is generally referred to as the indirect or vertical coupling between electrochemical heat generation and heat transport models. Vertical coupling is a reduced-order battery modeling approach where the electrochemical model is run independently or in a standalone mode [90]. The transient heat generation profile generated by the electrochemical model is applied as a transient heat source to the thermal model. Convection boundary conditions were applied on the cell side walls to simulate conditions inside the battery test chamber using heat transfer coefficient, h
f = 27.2 (W∙m−2∙K−1) and ambient temperature, T
amb = 298 K [91]. Simulation results were validated against experimental data and found to be in a reasonably good agreement ( Fig. 7b), thus justifying the proposition regarding the cell chemistry. The transient heat generation rate used in the simulations and cell surface temperature used to validate the model were derived from [89]. A 120 s square wave profile using ±1C (charge/discharge) load was adopted to generate the experimental data. The cell was cycled for 1 h, maintaining its state of charge at about 50%. Reference [89] could be referred for further details of the measurement procedure and operating conditions. Prior to the detailed simulations and model validation, grid independency test was carried out. Cell average, maximum, and minimum surface temperatures (i.e., T
s,avg , T
s,max , and T
s,min ) were used to optimize the grid size (Fig. 7a).

The model was subsequently used to simulate the effect of thermal anisotropy within the cell active zone. Two cases were simulated using the following set of parameters:
1.
Anisotropic thermal conductivity, k
c
P = 1075 [J∙kg−1∙K−1]
ρ = 2589 [kg∙m−3]
k
r = 0.4045 [W∙m−1∙K−1]
k
z = 44.4935 [W∙m−1∙K−1]
2.
Isotropic thermal conductivity, k

In case 1, all the parameters determined in the present work were used, representing the case of anisotropic thermal conductivity. In case 2, k = 22.4490 (W∙m−1∙K−1), that is, k = k
r = k
z was used as the isotropic thermal conductivity obtained by taking an average of the 2 components used in case 1. The other 2 parameters, that is, specific heat capacity (c
P ) and density (ρ) remained unchanged.

Contour plots of static temperature for both cases, that is, anisotropic and isotropic thermal conductivity, are shown in Fig. 8a. It can be seen that while the overall thermal gradient is comparable in both cases, the inner spatial distribution of temperature is markedly different. This difference is expected to increase at higher heat sources associated with higher applied currents, as in the case of EVs. Hence, to adequately capture thermal gradients within the cell using thermal models, anisotropic thermal conductivity must be used. In the line plots shown in Figs. 8c and 8d, lower temperature at the middle of the outer surface, in case of anisotropic k, is due to higher thermal resistance in the radial direction. Hence, heat transfer from the inner core to the cell outer surface takes longer, while heat reaching the surface gets immediately rejected due to convection, thus leaving the surface at a lower temperature. In case of isotropic k, heat reaches the surface quicker due to larger k
r as compared to the anisotropic case. The difference between the heat reaching the surface and heat rejection from the surface is smaller in the case of isotropic k as compared to that of the anisotropic k. Hence, temperature is distributed more uniformly at the surface in the case of isotropic k. Path (or line) plots of temperature along axial and radial directions for both cases are presented in Figs. 8c and 8d, respectively. It can be observed that in the case of isotropic k, temperature is distributed more uniformly as compared to that of the anisotropic k. Nonetheless, the case of isotropic k significantly underpredicts the temperature variations within the cell. Hence, anisotropic k gives a better insight into the thermal variations within the cell and significantly impacts the accuracy of model predictions.

Lumped thermal models used to characterize lithium-ion batteries are reduced-order models that are simple and computationally efficient as compared to the full-order battery models. The accuracy of the lumped thermal models depends on the accurate knowledge of thermophysical parameters. Numerous techniques are reported in the literature to find thermophysical parameters of lithium-ion battery components. The most important component of the lithium-ion battery, constituting a major part of the cell body, is the inner core, or so-called active, where most of the thermal changes occur. The present work proposes a simple and innovative approach to find thermophysical parameters of a lithium-ion cell active zone using experimental measurements combined with numerical simulations. Anisotropic thermal conductivity of dry and wetted electrode stack was measured experimentally. Specific heat capacity was then determined by fitting simulated transient temperature profiles to the experimental curves. Density was measured manually by taking the mass of the wetted specimen and dividing by the computed volume. Through-plane thermal conductivity (k
r ) of the wetted stack was found to be almost double that of the dry stack. Whereas, in the case of in-plane thermal conductivity (k
z ), the difference between wetted and dry specimens was negligible (Table 2). Averaged values of the 2 components were found to be k
r = 0.41 (W∙m−1∙K−1) and k
z = 45 (W∙m−1∙K−1) with about 4% and 2% respective errors. Specific heat capacity (c
p ) and density (ρ) were found to be 1075 ± 25 (J∙kg−1∙K−1) and 2589 (kg∙m−3), respectively.

In the end, measured parameters were compared with those reported in the published literature, determined using a variety of different approaches. Measured parameters were found to be confined well within the reported ranges for NMC chemistry. To further authenticate the chemistry of the stack used in the measurements, a 3D lumped thermal model was simulated using the measured thermophysical properties. Simulation results were validated against experimental data and found to be in good agreement. Simulations were also carried out to study the effect of thermal anisotropy on the temperature field across the cell domain. It was found that using isotropic thermal conductivity underpredicted the temperature field as compared to that when anisotropic thermal conductivity was used. Assuming isotropic thermal conductivity simplifies the model and significantly reduces computational burden; it could be a logical assumption at low current applications where higher thermal gradients are not expected. But at high current applications like e-mobility, more rigorous thermal models using anisotropic thermal conductivity must be used.

The present work is aimed at presenting a simpler and cost-effective approach to find thermal and physical parameters of lithium-ion battery, eliminating the need for expensive and sophisticated equipment. It is planned to extend this approach to incorporate the effect of operating temperature on the battery thermophysical parameters. The effect of applied pressure on the wetted stack is expected to influence the measured parameters; hence, it also needs to be investigated. Adding to the knowledge base for lithium-ion battery research, this work would contribute to the thermal characterization of advanced lithium-ion battery technologies, thus aiding in the development of efficient cooling and thermal management system designs.
